raw_text_excerpt: Nature Biomedical Engineering nature biomedical engineering https://doi.org/10.1038/s41551-026-01736-1 Article BoneCoT: multicentre validation of a whole-body skeleton foundation model for bone metastases guided by clinician-derived chain of thought Hui Zhao 1,5, Ruipeng Zhang 2,5, Zhiyu Wang 1,5, Yifeng Gu2, Shengyuan Xu 3, Sheng Wang 4 & Yuehua Li 2 Given the rising incidence of bone metastases, computed tomography is widely used worldwide as the initial imaging modality for their detection. Accurate diagnosis of bone metastases demands comprehensive evaluation, yet divergent interpretations among specialists can result in diagnostic discrepancies. In clinical practice, precision diagnosis of bone metastases necessitates multidisciplinary collaboration involving radiologists, pathologists and oncologists. Here, to meet the need for an automated tool that can deliver expert-level insights and predictions by jointly considering multidisciplinary information, we propose BoneCoT, a whole-body skeleton foundation model enhanced through a chain-of-thought (CoT) fine-tuning approach. We pretrained the model on 29.3 million computed tomography images from 30,267 patients across 12 skeletal sites and refined it over a graph of 26 clinically relevant tasks spanning diagnosis, complications, tumour type and biomarkers. Evaluated across 26 tasks and multicentre cohorts from 10 hospitals, BoneCoT outperformed state-of-the-art methods by 20% in area under the receiver operating characteristic curve. Critically, BoneCoT achieved a 40% area under the receiver operating characteristic curve improvement in distinguishing primary from metastatic lesions, significantly surpassing experienced radiologists. These findings show how clinician-derived reasoning can move artificial intelligence towards more integrated diagnostic assessment in complex disease. Metastasis occurs when cancer cells spread from the original solid cancer to other parts of the body. Among approximately 20 million new cancer cases diagnosed annually worldwide1, more than 50% of patients will develop metastatic disease2,3. As patients with cancer live longer, the increasing incidence of bone metastases (BM)4 is a growing concern. Among the most prevalent solid tumours in the general population, and when assessed among patients with distant metastatic disease, BM are frequently observed in lung (40%), breast (70%), prostate (85%) and kidney (40%) cancers2,5–7. Among patients with solid tumours, the mean time to BM is 700 days8. BM not only contributes substantially to cancer-related mortality but also leads to frequent skeletal-related events, such as pathological fracture, hypercalcaemia and spinal cord compression9–16, which are closely linked to loss of mobility and social functioning2,9,10. Moreover, BM are often misdiagnosed17, are associated with poor prognosis (average survival after BM diagnosis is 3–5 years for breast cancer and 1 year for lung cancer)2,5 and impose a substantial economic burden4,18,19. Received: 4 August 2025 Accepted: 2 June 2026 Published online: xx xx xxxx Check for updates A full list of affiliations appears at the end of the paper. e-mail: zhao-hui@sjtu.edu.cn; swang@cs.washington.edu; liyuehua77@sjtu.edu.cn Nature Biomedical Engineering Article https://doi.org/10.1038/s41551-026-01736-1 (ViT-g/14) architecture with a 518 × 518 input resolution. BoneFM was trained on a dataset of nearly 30 million CT images, spanning 12 skeletal locations (Fig. 1b ). BoneCoT then applies a clinician-curated multi - modal graph to jointly fine-tune 26 different BM-related tasks, covering genetic, pathological and clinical aspects of BM diagnosis. The key idea is to leverage this multimodal graph to simulate the multidisciplinary collaboration among clinicians with different expertise, allowing BoneCoT to simultaneously consider radiological, pathological, genetic and clinical information. During inference, BoneCoT uses CoT reasoning to jointly predict all 26 BM-related tasks without access to the true labels of any tasks. T o establish the effectiveness and generalizability of BoneCoT, we performed a comprehensive benchmark evaluation across 26 in-house and 10 multicentre datasets. This evaluation spanned three core diagnosis tasks—bone lesion detection, differentiation of benign from malignant lesions and classification of primary versus meta - static lesions—where BoneCoT was systematically compared with both existing foundation models 39,55 and radiologists with varying levels of expertise. Beyond these core tasks, our validation extended to a diverse spectrum of 23 additional tasks, encompassing 2 BM quality classifications, 5 BM-related complications, 10 tumour classifications and 6 biomarker predictions. Furthermore, we used interpretability analyses to distil the model’s decision-making process into clinically translatable guidelines for BM diagnosis. This work therefore intro - duces and validates BoneCoT, a whole-body skeleton foundation model that integrates pathological, genetic and clinical information with CT analysis via a CoT approach, presenting a new paradigm for the artificial intelligence-assisted diagnosis and prognosis of cancer metastasis. Evaluation of BoneCoT on BM-related prediction tasks BoneCoT was developed by training a whole-body skeleton CT foundation model (BoneFM) on 29,349,275 bone CT images from 30,267 patients across 12 skeletal locations (training dataset shown in Fig. 1b) and then fine-tuned using a clinically guided task relation graph (Fig. 1c); the fine-tuning, inference and testing workflow is summarized in Fig. 1d. We evaluated BoneCoT across 26 BM-related prediction tasks, organized into 5 categories—BM diagnosis (3 tasks), BM quality classification (2 tasks), clinical complications (5 tasks), tumour classification (10 tasks) and biomarker prediction (6 tasks)—all formulated as binary classification problems predicting the presence or absence of each target condition. BoneCoT achieved the best overall performance across the 26 tasks in Fig. 2a, with an average area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.67. This represents a 20% relative improvement over self-distillation with no labels (DINOv2) (AUROC of 0.56) and a 24% relative improvement over Merlin (0.54) (task-level P values were <0.001 for all comparisons except 2 biomarker tasks in BoneCoT versus Merlin: ALK (P = 0.509) and ER (P = 0.051); Supplementary Table 1). For the core BM diagnostic tasks, BoneCoT attained an AUROC of 0.85 for primary versus metastatic prediction (0.85 versus 0.58 for DINOv2 and 0.51 for Merlin, both P < 0.001) and 0.75 for benign versus malignant prediction (0.75 versus 0.61 for DINOv2 and 0.60 for Merlin, both P < 0.001), representing improvements of 47% and 23% compared with DINOv2, and 67% and 25% compared with Merlin, respectively. We show the correlation matrix among annotations for the 26 tasks in the multimodal dataset (Fig. 2b). The presence of notable correlations supports our hypothesis that leveraging intertask relationships (Fig. 1c) enhances predictive performance across diagnostic tasks. BoneCoT also attains the best performance on BM quality classification, BM-related complications, tumour classification and biomarker tasks. Compared with Merlin55, the convolutional neural network-based foundation model that is also pretrained by CT images, BoneCoT achieves an average 23% relative improvement on BM-related complications, 22% on BM quality classification, 24% on tumour classification Computed tomography (CT) scans are routinely used as the ini - tial step in diagnosing BM because they are fast, cost-effective and widely available2,20. Radiologists use CT images to sequentially identify lesions21–23, assess their benign or malignant nature24, and further determine whether they are primary or metastatic 25,26. Yet, this process is susceptible to critical delays. The initial detection of BM is often missed, either in patients presenting with bone pain without a known primary tumour2,6 or in patients with cancer for whom no standardized imaging tests are recommended for screening BM2,27. This frequently results in a postponed diagnosis, compromising patient outcomes. The accurate diagnosis of BM requires more comprehensive information, including pathology data to characterize the histological features of lesions, genetic data to assess the molecular basis of the tumour28,29 and clinical data to identify related complications30 and overall patient status. BM often co-occur with clinical complications, such as pathological fracture, hypercalcaemia and spinal cord compression, and can be more accurately diagnosed when biomarkers, such as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR)31,32, oestrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR)2,33, are available. Moreover, BM lesions present morphological similarities and overlapping imaging features across skeletal sites due to shared molecular mechanisms arising from interactions between the biological environment of the skeletal system and cancer cells2,34. This highlights an opportunity to learn generalizable bone patterns across the whole-body skeleton. Finally, manual interpretation and analysis of CT images are tedious and prone to human error 35,36, particularly when distinguishing primary from metastatic lesions, a challenge greatly exacerbated in high-volume, under-resourced settings. This difficulty is further compounded by the siloed nature of medical expertise, where the differing perspectives of specialists, such as oncologists and radiologists, can lead to diagnostic discrepancies. In clinical practice, addressing such complex diseases relies on multidisciplinary team (MDT) collaboration to integrate these diverse perspectives and overcome the limitations of any single specialty. Collectively, there is a pressing need for an automated tool using only the widely accessible CT scan that can deliver expert-level insights and predictions by jointly considering radiological, pathological, genetic and clinical information20,25,26,37. This need for a unified framework aligns with emerging para - digms in machine learning, namely foundation models 38,39 and chain-of-thought (CoT) methods 40–42. Foundation models exploit self-supervised learning to learn high-quality representations from large-scale unannotated data, which can then be adapted to vari - ous downstream tasks43–46. Recent advances such as MedVersa47 and VILA-M3 (ref. 48) have demonstrated the potential of unified medical foundation models. However, these frameworks aim to cover a very broad range of imaging modalities and task scales, and their current performance is limited by the biased distribution of publicly available data. As such, they are not yet optimized for clinically specialized applications, such as BM diagnosis. In parallel, previous deep learning work in bone oncology has primarily focused on single-task solutions, such as lesion segmentation and differentiation between primary and metastatic bone tumours49–51. We hypothesize that a foundation model trained on CT images from diverse skeleton sites can learn generalizable features for diagnosing BM. Moreover, CoT has been built on top of the foundation model to enable logical reasoning ability by the model41,52–54. We hypothesize that CoT techniques allow us to incorporate clinical, pathological and genetic information into the whole-body CT model, thereby enabling a more comprehensive and accurate diagnosis of BM. Here, we propose BoneCoT, a CoT-enhanced whole-body skeleton foundation model for BM diagnosis. We collected data from 2013 to 2023, spanning over 10 years, with comprehensive genetic, pathological and clinical information, representing one of the largest datasets for BM studies (Fig. 1a). As a first step, we trained the base CT foundation model, bone foundation model (BoneFM), using a Vision Transformer Nature Biomedical Engineering Article https://doi.org/10.1038/s41551-026-01736-1 and 19% on biomarker prediction. Compared with DINOv2, BoneCoT demonstrates a 22% improvement on BM-related complications, 31% on BM quality classification, 19% on tumour classification and 14% on biomarker prediction. The contribution of integrating bone lesion quality classification is evident in two challenging cases (Fig. 2c,d). For these instances, which were misclassified by the baseline models, BoneCoT provided the correct prediction. Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) analy - sis identified features of lesion morphology—specifically, osteolytic and osteoblastic characteristics—as the most critical contributors to accurate BM identification. For these specific cases, SHAP analysis identified osteolytic characteristics as the predominant contributor in Fig. 2c, with a SHAP value of 0.54, and osteoblastic features as the predominant contributor in Fig. 2d, with a SHAP value of 1.85. Comparison of the diagnostic performance of BoneCoT and radiologists in predicting BM After confirming the superior performance of BoneCoT over other machine learning methods, we next compared BoneCoT with radiologists to assess its potential clinical utility. BoneCoT achieved an F1 score of 0.78 for primary versus metastatic prediction, whereas the average F1 scores for the senior and junior radiologist groups were 0.66 and 0.47, respectively (Fig. 3a,b). raw_text_excerpt: Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Medical Image Analysis journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/media LMGDM: A Lesion-aware Mutual Guidance Diffusion Model with attenuation prior constraint for self-attenuation correction of whole-body PET Shengjun Li a,b, Kaicong Sun b,∗, Caiwen Jiang b, Zaixin Ou b, Ruilong Dan b, Qianjin Feng a,∗, Dinggang Shen b,c,d,∗∗ a School of Biomedical Engineering, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, 510515, China b School of Biomedical Engineering and State Key Laboratory of Advanced Medical Materials and Devices, ShanghaiTech University, Shanghai, 201210, China c Shanghai United Imaging Intelligence Co., Ltd., Shanghai, 200230, China d Shanghai Clinical Research and Trial Center, Shanghai, 201210, China A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Whole-body PET Self-attenuation correction Mutual guidance Lesion-aware Attenuation prior A B S T R A C T Attenuation correction is critical for PET imaging to correctly reflect physiological activity. Recent studies perform PET self-attenuation correction, enabling attenuation correction from PET data itself instead of using additional CT or MRI. These methods either predict attenuation-corrected PET (AC-PET) from non-attenuationcorrected PET (NAC-PET) directly or synthesize intermediate CT images to guide PET attenuation correction. However, cross-modality synthesis of CT from NAC-PET is challenging, especially for small yet clinically important lesions. To address these challenges, we propose the Lesion-aware Mutual Guidance Diffusion Model (LMGDM), a coarse-to-fine dual-branch diffusion model that jointly performs PET attenuation correction and CT synthesis, with particular focus on lesion regions. Specifically, we first generate coarse predictions of both AC-PET and CT using individual residual diffusion models. Subsequently, the coarse AC-PET and CT are jointly refined by a proposed dual-branch mutual guidance module to enable feature fusion of the AC-PET and CT branches. Moreover, a lesion-aware refinement module is embedded into the PET branch, encouraging the network to focus on regions with pathologically high uptake rather than physiologically high uptake. In addition, an attenuation prior learned from real CT images is also introduced to further enhance the fidelity of the synthesized AC-PET and CT. Extensive evaluation on eight data centers demonstrates strong superiority of our LMGDM over the state-of-the-art methods. 1. Introduction Positron emission tomography (PET) is an advanced imaging technique that uses radioactive tracers to reflect physiological activity (Wang and Maurer, 2005; Decazes et al., 2021), playing an essential role in the diagnosis of cancer, neurology, and cardiology (Phelps, 2000; Rohren et al., 2004; Fletcher et al., 2008; Wang et al., 2020). During PET imaging, due to the photoelectric effect and Compton scattering, the photons undergo attenuation as they traverse the human body before reaching external detectors (Rong et al., 2023), leading to issues such as degraded image quality, underestimation of tissue metabolic activity, and quantitative errors in lesion assessment. Therefore, attenuation correction is required to recover the accurate distribution of the tracer within the body. In current clinical practice, PET attenuation correction typically relies on an additional structural ∗ Corresponding authors. ∗∗ Corresponding author at: School of Biomedical Engineering and State Key Laboratory of Advanced Medical Materials and Devices, ShanghaiTech University, Shanghai, 201210, China. E-mail addresses: sunkc@shanghaitech.edu.cn (K. Sun), fengqj99@smu.edu.cn (Q. Feng), Dinggang.Shen@gmail.com (D. Shen). scan, most often Computed Tomography (CT), to obtain an attenuation map (𝜇-map) of human tissues, increasing imaging costs and radiation exposure (Prieto et al., 2021). A promising solution is to perform PET self-attenuation correction directly from PET data without additional scans. In recent years, PET self-attenuation correction has been extensively studied and can be broadly categorized into one-step attenuation correction methods (Shiri et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2024; Dong et al., 2020; Li et al., 2024; Cho and Lee, 2023; Chen et al., 2025) and two-step attenuation correction methods (Liu et al., 2018; Armanious et al., 2020; Dong et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2023; Toyonaga et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2024). One-step attenuation correction methods attempt to learn an end-to-end mapping from non-attenuationcorrected PET (NAC-PET) to attenuation-corrected PET (AC-PET). Although straightforward in design and easy to implement, these methods https://doi.org/10.1016/j.media.2026.104169 Received 26 November 2025; Received in revised form 2 May 2026; Accepted 10 June 2026 Medical Image Analysis 113 (2026) 104169 Available online 15 June 2026 1361-8415/© 2026 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies. S. Li et al. model the complex photon attenuation implicitly, often resulting in less interpretable and overly smoothed AC-PET images. In contrast, two-step attenuation correction methods first synthesize CT or 𝜇-map from the NAC-PET and then use it to guide PET attenuation correction. By incorporating anatomical priors into the process, these methods typically deliver enhanced performance and better interpretability. Nevertheless, existing two-step methods face two main limitations. First, inter-modality synthesis from NAC-PET to CT is inherently challenging and the errors in the synthesized CT will propagate to PET attenuation correction. Since most two-step methods treat CT synthesis and PET attenuation correction in a sequential manner, the errors in the synthesized CT do not have chance to be corrected. Second, clinically critical lesion regions with pathologically high uptake often occupy only a small portion of the image compared to the normal tissues with physiologically high-uptake such as the brain, kidneys, liver, and bladder (Boellaard et al., 2015; Kostakoglu et al., 2004). Consequently, attenuation correction of these lesions is distracted by physiologically high-uptake regions, which ultimately compromises the performance of PET attenuation correction. These limitations highlight the need for a more effective and lesion-aware self-attenuation correction method. To address the above challenges, we propose a Lesion-aware Mutual Guidance Diffusion Model (LMGDM) for whole-body PET selfattenuation correction. Instead of treating CT synthesis as an isolated intermediate task, the proposed method adopts a coarse-to-fine dualbranch strategy to jointly optimize PET attenuation correction and CT synthesis. Specifically, two residual diffusion models (RDM) (Yue et al., 2023) are used to generate the coarse AC-PET and CT separately in the coarse stage. In the fine stage, the coarse AC-PET and CT images are jointly and progressively refined within a dual-branch RDM framework, where a dual-branch mutual guidance module (DMGM) is introduced to facilitate feature interaction between the AC-PET and CT branches. To improve the recovery of clinically important pathological uptake regions, we further introduce a lesion-aware refinement module (LRM) to integrate an estimated lesion map as guidance for PET attenuation correction. Additionally, an attenuation prior module (APM) is developed to further regularize the synthesized CT and AC-PET using prior knowledge learned from real CT. Extensive experiments on eight datasets demonstrate that the proposed method can significantly outperform the state-of-the-art methods quantitatively and qualitatively. 2. Related work In the last decade, many studies have explored PET self-attenuation correction using deep learning (DL)-based models (Lee, 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Zaharchuk and Davidzon, 2021; Matsubara et al., 2022; Chen and Liu, 2023; Krokos et al., 2023). These approaches can be categorized into one-step attenuation correction methods and two-step attenuation correction methods. 2.1. One-step attenuation correction methods One-step attenuation correction methods aim to learn an end-to-end mapping from NAC-PET to AC-PET. In early work, Shiri et al. (2020) proposed Deep-JASC, a deep residual network (He et al., 2016) that predicts whole-body AC-PET from NAC-PET. Guo et al. (2022) introduced a domain knowledge-integrated decomposition-based deep learning method that decomposes the task into anatomy-independent textures and anatomy-dependent ones, easing the prediction of AC-PET from NAC-PET. The advent of Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) (Goodfellow et al., 2014) significantly advanced this field. Sun et al. (2024) proposed a CGAN-based model (Mirza and Osindero, 2014), and Dong et al. (2020) introduced a CycleGAN-based model (Zhu et al., 2017), both demonstrating the feasibility of one-step generation of AC-PET without additional structural information. Later, Li et al. (2024) incorporated structural priors from five anatomical sites (head and neck, chest, abdomen, pelvis, and extremities) into the discriminator to enhance anatomical consistency of generated AC-PET. Recently, Diffusion model (DM) has excelled in medical image generation (Ho et al., 2020; Kazerouni et al., 2023; Meng et al., 2024) due to their remarkable capability to model complex data distributions. Cho and Lee (2023) introduced a likelihood-based Conditional Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (CDDPM) for whole-body PET attenuation correction, showing superior accuracy over GAN-based methods by incorporating NAC-PET feature representations. More recently, Chen et al. (2025) proposed the Multi-view Averaging Diffusion Model (MADM), a 2.5D diffusion model that ensures 3D consistency by training independent 2.5D models for axial, coronal, and sagittal views, respectively. Although one-step attenuation correction methods can produce visually plausible AC-PET images, the absence of explicit anatomical guidance makes the generated AC-PET prone to inaccurate standardized uptake value (SUV) in the generated AC-PET, particularly in regions with anatomical variations or with lesions. 2.2. Two-step attenuation correction methods To mitigate this issue, two-step attenuation correction methods were proposed to adopt an alternative strategy by synthesizing anatomical images, typically CT or 𝜇-map, to guide PET attenuation correction. Liu et al. (2018) proposed a deep convolutional autoencoder network to generate CT from NAC-PET for brain PET attenuation correction. However, this study focused only on the brain and was implemented using a 2D framework, without validation on whole-body PET images. Later, Armanious et al. (2020) extended the strategy to whole-body PET using a GAN-based framework and used it for subsequent PET attenuation correction. The results indicate that generating CT images from NAC-PET and performing PET attenuation correction is feasible. However, the generated CT images remain blurry, and the generated anatomical details appear irregular, indicating that this method still faces challenges in areas with complex anatomical structures. Dong et al. (2019) further proposed a 3D CycleGAN with self-attention for whole-body CT generation from NAC-PET. Their method improved the representation of soft tissue, lung, and bone structures and achieved low PET quantification error. However, their method primarily focused on NAC-PET-to-CT conversion, without explicitly distinguishing attention caused by the pathologically and physiologically high-uptake regions. Recognizing the inherent difficulty of this cross-modality synthesis, more sophisticated frameworks were developed. Pan et al. (2023) proposed the Anatomical Skeleton Enhancement Generation (ASEG) framework to generate CT images from NAC-PET for PET attenuation correction. Particularly, the framework decouples CT generation into two sequential tasks, by first predicting an anatomical skeleton from NAC-PET, and then using this skeleton as a structural prior to render tissue details. Beyond CT synthesis, several methods have focused on generating 𝜇-map. For example, Toyonaga et al. (2022) developed a deep learning framework based on time-of-flight (TOF) PET data, which used activity and attenuation maps from MLAA to generate 𝜇-map for PET attenuation correction. Moreover, Zhou et al. (2024) proposed a Population-Prior-Aided Over-Under-Representation Network (POURNet), a cascade deep learning framework enabling 𝜇-map generation from low-dose PET for PET attenuation correction. In summary, existing methods reveal a clear trade-off. The onestep methods avoid the inherent challenge of CT synthesis, but they lack explicit anatomical guidance and compromise SUV fidelity. In contrast, the two-step methods improve interpretability by introducing anatomical priors, but they remain vulnerable to cross-modality synthesis errors and typically optimize CT generation and PET attenuation correction in a sequential manner. Moreover, neither category explicitly addresses the imbalance between small pathological lesions and large physiologically high-uptake organs. These observations motivate our design of a coarse-to-fine dual-branch framework with mutual PET-CT guidance and lesion-aware PET attenuation correction. Medical Image Analysis 113 (2026) 104169 2 S. Methods The overview of our proposed LMGDM is shown in Fig. 1. Given an NAC-PET input, the first stage employs two Residual Diffusion Models (RDM) to individually generate coarse AC-PET and coarse CT. Then, these coarse images are jointly refined in the second stage. The following subsections describe each key component in detail. 3.1. Coarse-to-fine PET self-attenuation correction Synthesizing CT directly from NAC-PET is a challenging task due to limited anatomical details in NAC-PET. To alleviate the difficulty, we propose a coarse-to-fine learning strategy. In the coarse stage, two independent generative models are adopted to generate coarse AC-PET and coarse CT from NAC-PET. Based on this, in the fine stage, dual-branch mutual guidance and lesion-aware refinement are integrated, such that coarse AC-PET and coarse CT can be synergistically optimized. Since conventional diffusion models usually suffer from prohibitive inference time due to a large number of required sampling steps, herein we leverage the RDM (Yue et al., 2023) as the backbone for both the coarse and fine stages, which directly transforms the source distribution, instead of random Gaussian noise, to the target distribution and largely reduces the amount of sampling steps. Specifically, for the PET branch, we denote the NAC-PET and AC-PET images as 𝑦0 and 𝑥0, respectively. The residual between NAC-PET and AC-PET is represented as 𝑒0, i.e., 𝑒0 = 𝑦0 − 𝑥0. A shifting sequence {𝜂𝑡}𝑇 𝑡=1 is adopted, which monotonically increases with the timestep 𝑡 and satisfies 𝜂1 → 0 and 𝜂𝑇 → 1. The transition distribution is then formulated based on this shifting sequence as follows: 𝑞(𝑥𝑡|𝑥𝑡−1, 𝑦0) =  (𝑥𝑡; 𝑥𝑡−1 + 𝛼𝑡𝑒0, 𝜅2𝛼𝑡𝑰), 𝑡 = 1, …, 𝑇, (1) where 𝛼𝑡 = 𝜂𝑡 − 𝜂𝑡−1 for 𝑡 > 1 and 𝛼1 = 𝜂1. The 𝜅 acts as a shiftstep control constant, determining the magnitude of the residual shift between 𝑦0 and 𝑥0. The joint probability distribution in the forward diffusion process can be defined as: 𝑞(𝑥𝑡|𝑥0, 𝑦0) =  (𝑥𝑡; 𝑥0 + 𝜂𝑡𝑒0, 𝜅2𝜂𝑡𝑰), 𝑡 = 1, 2, …, 𝑇. (2) The reverse process restores AC-PET 𝑥0 from the noisy NAC-PET 𝑥𝑇. Index Terms — Alzheimer’s disease diagnosis, incomplete multimodal learning, state space models, medical image analysis, multimodal fusion. I. I NTRODUCTION A LZHEIMER’s disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder, prominently manifested through memory impairment, cognitive decline, and deteriorating self-care abilities, posing signiﬁcant personal and societal challenges worldwide [1], [2], [3]. The absence of curative therapies for AD accentuates the importance of early detection and intervention, particularly at the stage of Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI), which is recognized as an intermediate transitional phase between normal aging and dementia. Timely identiﬁcation and management of MCI can signiﬁcantly delay disease progression and improve long-term outcomes for patients and their families [4]. The rapid advancement in multimodal biomedical data acquisition, including structural magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), positron emission tomography (PET), cerebrospinal ﬂuid (CSF) biomarkers, and diverse clinical assessment data (CAD), has paved the way for more holistic data-driven AD diagnosis [5]. These heterogeneous data sources, when eﬀectively integrated, can oﬀer a multidimensional understanding of disease mechanisms and enhance diagnostic reliability. Consequently, multimodal AI frameworks have become increasingly prominent in neurodegenerative disease research, enabling joint analysis that leverages complementary information from distinct modalities [6], [7]. Despite these advancements, several critical barriers persist in practical clinical applications. First, multimodal datasets are often incomplete: missing data can arise from high acquisition costs, patient dropout, radiation exposure concerns, or logistical issues [8], [9]. Traditional approaches that discard incomplete samples or rely solely on fully observed data inevitably reduce cohort size and model generalizability [10]. To tackle this, recent research has explored imputation, subspace learning, and knowledge distillation strategies [11], [12], yet these solutions are often limited by the quality of imputed data and the complex heterogeneity of medical modalities. 1558-254X © 2026 IEEE. LIU et al.: MULTI-VIEW HILBERT CURVE-BASED HIERARCHICAL INFORMATION AGGREGATION 3949 Notably, generative models such as GANs and autoencoders can introduce redundant or misleading representations, further complicating downstream analysis. Another formidable challenge lies in the architectural limitations of current AI models. While three-dimensional convolutional neural networks (3D CNNs) are widely used for imaging data, they are often constrained by parameter ineﬃciency and limited receptive ﬁelds, hindering their ability to model long-range spatial dependencies [13]. Transformer-based architectures, although powerful in capturing global context, incur prohibitive computational costs as data dimensionality increases. This is particularly problematic in the context of high-resolution 3D medical images, where quadratic complexity with respect to sequence length severely restricts scalability and clinical deployment. Structured State Space Models (SSMs), exempliﬁed by the recent Mamba family of models, have emerged as a promising alternative, oﬀering linear-time sequence modeling and eﬃcient long-range dependency capture [14], [15], [16]. However, extending such architectures to irregular, heterogeneous, and incomplete multimodal medical datasets remains largely unexplored. In this work, we propose a comprehensive solution to these pressing issues by developing a Hierarchical Information Aggregation and Dynamic late fusion (HI-AD) framework tailored for incomplete multimodal AD diagnosis. To be speciﬁc, we address these issues with a heterogeneous multimodal framework, HI-AD, designed to adapt to arbitrary subsets of modalities at both training and inference. First, we introduce a Hilbert curve-based multi-view Mamba block (HMamba) for 3D imaging. By scanning volumes along multiple Hilbert curve orientations, our proposed HMamba preserves locality better than simple line or zig-zag traversals, and aggregates complementary context via voxel-wise dynamic weights. Building on HMamba, a hierarchical spatial feature extractor (HSFE) is proposed to interleave residual 3D convolutions with SSM-based long-range modeling across multiple resolutions, unifying local detail and global dependency while keeping computation eﬃcient. In addition, to coordinate semantic alignment with modality speciﬁcity, we formulate a mutual information driven objective in a shared embedding space, and couple a dynamic mid-level mixture-of-experts with a hybrid late-fusion strategy that eﬀectively leverages modality-speciﬁc predictions in entire decision-making. This strategy counteracts the common training imbalance where “easy-to-ﬁt” modalities dominate optimization, thereby reducing the risk of modality collapse and improving robustness under missing-modality regimes. In addition, we propose a conﬁdence-guided multimodal dynamic aggregation mechanism, by proactively evaluating the information level for each modality of each sample, which ﬁnes the granularity of multimodal information balance to the instance level. Our contributions are threefold: • We develop a uniﬁed multimodal diagnostic framework capable of robustly handling arbitrary patterns of missing modalities, eﬀectively leveraging information from structural MRI, PET, biomarkers, and clinical assessment scores. • To address spatial discontinuity and eﬃcient information extraction in 3D image encoding, we introduce a multiview Hilbert curve-based Mamba block, alongside a hierarchical spatial feature extraction module. This combination enables the model to capture both ﬁne-grained local features and long-range global dependencies. • We design a mutual information-maximizing semantic learning objective with an active conﬁdence evaluation mechanism, which prevents training collapse while eﬀectively extracting multimodal semantic information. Through extensive experimentation on large-scale heterogeneous clinical datasets, we demonstrate that our HI-AD framework sets a new benchmark for incomplete multimodal AD diagnosis, substantially outperforming existing approaches under a variety of realistic missing-data scenarios. This work thus provides a scalable and clinically practical foundation for future AI-driven neurodegenerative disease diagnostics. II. P RELIMINARY.A. Incomplete Multimodal AD Diagnosis In practice, however, one or more modalities are frequently unavailable due to radiation risks and costs, invasiveness, and limited availability or acquisition logistics. Incompleteness can occur in both training and inference, with arbitrary patterns across subjects. Existing approaches to learning under such incompleteness broadly fall into two families: generative completion and imputation-free learning. 1) Generative completion. This line uses learned translators to synthesize missing modalities from available ones, most commonly via Generative Adversarial Network (GANs), Variational Autoencoder (V AEs) or diﬀusion models, before applying standard multimodal fusion [17]. In AD imaging, examples include MRI-PET synthesis or ﬁlling missing channels from imaging-derived embeddings [18], and recent diﬀusion-style completion for medical images and tokens [19]. Although completion can enlarge the eﬀective multimodal dataset, two practical issues are prominent: (i) supervision gap—when complete modalities are scarce in training, it is diﬃcult to guarantee the ﬁdelity of synthesized data and to calibrate downstream gains; (ii) computational overhead—complicated generators over 3D neuroimaging are expensive to train and deploy, and errors from imperfect synthesis can propagate and bias the classiﬁer. Empirically, synthetic signals may introduce redundant or misleading cues, especially under strong heterogeneity between 3D images and unstructured data. 2) Imputation-free (missingness-aware) learning. Instead of generating incomplete modalities, models are trained to operate on the observed subset. Representative strategies include: (i) modality masking to expose the network to incompleteness during training [20], [21], or availabilitygated fusion with binary masks so only present modalities contribute to encoding, alignment, and losses [22], [23]; (ii) shared–speciﬁc representation learning (distribution alignment for shared features and modality Authorized licensed use limited to: Chinese University of Hong Kong. In AD studies, such imputation-free designs consistently reduce test-time degradation under arbitrary missing patterns and avoid synthesis bias at substantially lower computational cost..B. State Space Models State space models (SSMs) oﬀer linear-time sequence modeling with strong long-range dependency capture, making them attractive alternatives to attention for high-dimensional medical data. Recent progress starts from HiPPO [26], which provides a principled way to represent continuous-time dynamics, followed by S4 [27] that enables parallel training via discretization and convolutional formulations. Subsequently, Mamba [14] further streamlines the architecture with selective state updates, improving eﬃciency and hardware parallelism. Currently, some vision adaptation methods extend SSMs from 1D sequences to images and volumes, eﬀectively sidestepping the quadratic complexity of Transformers while preserving global context modeling. These developments make SSMbased backbones well-suited for 3D multimodal neuroimaging, where long-range spatial dependencies and computational eﬃ- ciency are both critical. A central challenge is that naive raster scans disrupt spatial neighborhoods, harming local structure crucial for medical images. Our design addresses this with Hilbert curve-based serialization and multi-view fusion, described next..C. Hilbert Curve for 3D Brain Image Data Existing Vision Mamba approaches usually serialize structured 2D/3D inputs into 1D sequences via bi-directional cross or continuous scanning methods [28], [29], [30]. These simple scans inevitably break intrinsic spatial structure, pushing spatially adjacent voxels far apart in the sequence from 3D image. The resulting spatial discontinuity hinders the model’s ability to learn local structures and long-range dependencies, ultimately constraining diagnostic performance in medical imaging. To address this, we adopt a 3D Hilbert curve to scan brain volumes. In contrast to conventional scanning strategies [31], [32]. Hilbert curves are space-ﬁlling fractals that map a multidimensional grid to a 1D sequence while preserving locality to a high degree. For a cubic voxel lattice of size 2N× 2N× 2N, the 3D Hilbert curve of order N recursively subdivides the cube and visits all 2 3N voxels through rotations and reﬂections, maintaining continuity across sub-cubes. Fig. 1 shows the 3D Hilbert curve with orders N∈{1, 2, 3, 4}. Let HN:{0,..., 2N− 1}3→{0,..., 23N− 1} denote the mapping from a voxel (x, y, z) to its sequence index h = HN(x, y, z). Compared with raster or zig-zag scans, the Hilbert trajectory better preserves adjacency relationships, meaning that voxels close in 3D tend to remain nearby in the serialized order. This locality-preserving property reduces spatial “tearing” during serialization, helping state-space encoders capture both local structure and long-range spatial context when processing 3D brain images..D. Understanding the complex developmental processes is essential for elucidating the biological bases of cognition and behavior, as well as the mechanisms underlying aging and neurodegenerative diseases. Neuroimaging has enabled the mapping of nonlinear, age-related changes in brain morphology, microstructure, and connectivity from gestation to senescence. However, we lack a uni ﬁed understanding of interplay across multimodal neuroimaging measures, structure – function coupling, and the cellular and molecular drivers of network reorganization. In this review, we synthesize current evidence to provide a multiscale MRIderived account of structural and functional brain development across the human lifespan, highlight key conceptual and methodological gaps, and outline priorities for future research. The human brain undergoes continual development and remodeling throughout the lifespan. This process begins in the third gestational week and continues into adulthood. After reaching maturity, the brain gradually undergoes structural and functionaldegeneration. A myriad of neurobiological processes orchestrate early brain development, shaping later neurocognitive function and susceptibility to neurodegeneration. Consequently, brain development is a heterogeneous process, characterized by macrostructural and microstructural alterations across the lifespan. Advances in structural MRI (sMRI), diffusion MRI (dMRI), and functional MRI (fMRI) have enabled increasingly detailed characterization of brain development across the lifespan. However, theﬁeld remains challenged by limited longitudinal MRI data, inconsistent computational methodologies, and nonbiological confounds in acquired MRI data. Understanding the complex developmental processes is essential for elucidating the biological bases of cognition and behavior, as well as the mechanisms underlying aging and neurodegenerative diseases. Neuroimaging has enabled the mapping of nonlinear, age-related changes in brain morphology, microstructure, and connectivity from gestation to senescence. However, we lack a uni ﬁed understanding of interplay across multimodal neuroimaging measures, structure – function coupling, and the cellular and molecular drivers of network reorganization. In this review, we synthesize current evidence to provide a multiscale MRIderived account of structural and functional brain development across the human lifespan, highlight key conceptual and methodological gaps, and outline priorities for future research. The human brain undergoes continual development and remodeling throughout the lifespan. This process begins in the third gestational week and continues into adulthood. After reaching maturity, the brain gradually undergoes structural and functionaldegeneration. A myriad of neurobiological processes orchestrate early brain development, shaping later neurocognitive function and susceptibility to neurodegeneration. Consequently, brain development is a heterogeneous process, characterized by macrostructural and microstructural alterations across the lifespan. Advances in structural MRI (sMRI), diffusion MRI (dMRI), and functional MRI (fMRI) have enabled increasingly detailed characterization of brain development across the lifespan. However, theﬁeld remains challenged by limited longitudinal MRI data, inconsistent computational methodologies, and nonbiological confounds in acquired MRI data. In this review, we highlight current research on brain development across the lifespan, emphasizing how different MRI modalities capture neurodevelopmental processes at multiple spatial and temporal scales. We also discuss recent advances in computational methods that facilitate the processing and analysis of brain MRIs, thereby deepening our understanding of human brain development across the lifespan. Brain morphology Human brain development is charact erized by morphological changes throughout the lifespan. Global and regional variations in brain structure contribute to individual differen ces in cognition and brain function 1. Charting normative patterns of brain growth across the lifespan is essential for identifying atypical deviations associated with brain disorders. Advances in sMRI, together with the recent availability of large-scale lifespan datasets2–7, now enable precise mapping of macroscopic brain development. These efforts have revealed nonlinear, region-speciﬁc, and heterochronous patterns of structural maturation and aging, underpinned by cellular and molecular processes such as neurogenesis, synaptogenesis, synaptic pruning, oligodendrogenesis, and myelination 8. Brain development in early life is marked by rapid increases in brain size, greater cortical folding complexity, and pronounced changes in tissue contrast (Fig. 1). The infant brain reaches ~ 64% of adult brain volume within the ﬁrst three months9.D u r i n gt h eﬁrst postnatal year, gray matter volume increases by ~ 97% and white matter volume by ~ 93% 10.A c r o s s later development, different brain tissues follow distinct trajectories. Cerebral gray matter volume peaks in midd le childhood, whereas subcortical gray matter volume peaks during adolescence11. In contrast, white matter volume increases more gradually and continues to rise into young adulthood 11. Cerebrospinalﬂuid (CSF) volume increases across the lifespan, with modest expansion in early life followed by accelerated growth in late adulthood. This increase largely reﬂects brain atrophy with aging, which leads to enlargement of the ventricles and subarachnoid spaces in response to reduction in the overall brain volume 12. Other brain structures also show characteristic age-related volume changes. Extra-cortical structures, including the subcortex, cerebellu m, and brainstem, generally follow inverted U-shaped trajectories, with rapid early life growth followed by gradual decline 13. The cerebellum grows the fastest, with its volume more than doubling during theﬁrst 90 days, whereas the hippocampus grows the slowest, increasing by only 47% 9. The basal ganglia (caudate nucleus, nucleus accumbens, putamen, and globus pallidus) increase in volume until roughly the ﬁrst decade of life and then decline linearly with age 13.I n contrast, the thalamus and limbic structures (hippocampus and amygdala) continue to grow into young adulthood, remain relatively stable through 1Department of Radiology, The University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, Chapel Hill, NC, USA. 2Biomedical Research Imaging Center (BRIC), The University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, Chapel Hill, NC, USA. e-mail: sahar967@email.unc.edu; ptyap@med.unc.edu Communications Biology | (2026) 9:965 1 1234567890():,; 1234567890():,; midlife, and decline after the sixth decade. One study of individuals aged 5–90 years reported annual gray matter decline rates of 0.51% in the amygdala, 0.314% in the putamen, 0.19% in the caudate nucleus, and 0.155% in the cerebellum 14. The brainstem reaches its maximal volume at approximately 51 years of age. Analyses of cortical regional volumes using the Desikan-Killiany parcellation further show heterogeneity in developmental timing. For example, the primary sensory regions reach peak volume earliest, during toddlerhood, whereas the association regions peak much later 11. Cerebellar lobules also exhibit het erogeneous volumetric changes across the lifespan. Postnatal lobular development is characterized by rapid volume increases during the ﬁrst year of life, with prolonged growth in h i g h e r - o r d e rl o b u l e ss u c ha sC r u sI, Crus II, and VIIB, whereas lobule X shows an earlier transition to slowed growth at approximately 200 days after birth 15. During childhood and adolescence,the greatest volumetric expansion occurs in the corpus medullare16. Cerebellar gray matter lobules also display spatially organized de velopmental patterns along the anterior–posterior axis. The anterior lob e, associated with sensorimotor functions, matures earlier than the posterior lobe, associated with higherorder functions16,17. From 20 to 80 years of age, several cerebellar regions exhibit signiﬁcant age-related volume decline, including parts of the anterior lobe (right I–III and V), posterior lobe (bilateral VI, Crus I, Crus II, VIIB, and IX), the ﬂocculonodular lobe (right X), and vermis X18. Another study reported the steepest age-related volumetric decline in the central zone of the cerebellum 19. Progressive cerebellar volume loss has been linked to functional impairments in both cognitive and motor domains20–22. During early brain development23, complex gyriﬁcation transforms the initially smooth cerebral surface into a highly convoluted structure. This process occurs across multiple spatial scales: (i) at a global scale, the cerebral surface is divided into two hemispheres; and (ii) at a ﬁner scale, primary, secondary, and tertiary folds progressively emerge. As the brain develops across the lifespan, regions also c hange in shape and orientation. For example, the narrow sulcal spaces observed in the infant brain widen with aging24,25. In addition, cortical gyri and sulci exhibit substantial morphological variability both within and across individuals. Cortical thickness, deﬁned as the vertex-wise distance between the white and pial surfaces, has been extensively studied across the lifespan. The cerebral cortex is relatively thick during infancy, reaching a peak at approximately 1.7 years of age, and then undergoes progressive thinning from childhood through adolescence and adulthood 11,26. These changes are not spatially uniform but instead follow a gradient along the sensorimotor–association axis27. Primary sensory and motor cortices exhibit earlierand more rapid reductions in cortical thickness before childhood, whereasheteromodal and paralimbic cortices mature more gradually and continue to thin into the early 20s28,29. In terms of surface area, the cerebr al cortex expands dramatically during theﬁrst postnatal year, nearly doubling on average, with particularly pronounced increases in the temporal, parietal, prefrontal, occipital, and sensorimotor cortices30. Surface area continues to increase during the second year of life, although at a slower rate, and this expansion persists into early childhood in a region-speciﬁc manner. The largest increases in surface area, ranging from 87% to 108%, have beenobserved in the lingual, fusiform, and rostral anterior cingulate gyri 31. Structural networks Structural brain networks provide a systems-level framework for understanding how neural architecture andinter-regional communication in the human brain emerge, reorganize, and d ecline across the lifespan. These networks are typically constructed bycombining dMRI-based tractography, which maps white matter ﬁber pathways (Fig. 1), with sMRI-based parcellation that delineates distinct brain regions35. In this framework, brain regions are represented as nodes and white matter tracts as edges connecting them. The strength of a connection between two regions can be quantiﬁed using measures such as streamline count, streamline density, or along-tract microstructural properties 36.T h e s em e a s u r e sd eﬁne a weighted adjacency matrix, to which graph-theoretical methods are applied to characterize the organization and properties of structural brain networks 37. Leveraging these network construction tools, a growing body of work has characterized nonlinear changes instructural brain networks driven by multiple neurobiological processes, i ncluding synaptogenesis, synaptic pruning, axonal growth, myelination, and apoptosis38,39. Network strength, local efﬁciency, and clustering coefﬁcient increase across the lifespan, while global efﬁciencyﬂuctuates with its peak occurring around 29 years followed by continuous decline through late life40–42. Small-worldness demonstrates an inverse pattern, reaching a minimum in young adulthood followed by a nonlinear increase40. During adolescence, structural brain network modules become progressively more segregated, driven by strengthening of withinmodule connections and weakening of between-module connections 43. These reconﬁgurations are concentrated at hub edges, enabling networks to achieve greater modular specialization while maintaining efﬁcient global integration40,44,45. Wiring distance analyses further highlight the interplay between integration and segregation: although both shortand long-range connections strengthen during development and weaken with aging, the relative contribution of short-range connectivity decreases as the brain matures 46,47. Functional networks Functional brain networks consist of groups of brain regions that exhibit co- ﬂuctuating activity, typically measur ed using the blood-oxygen-leveldependent (BOLD) signal in fMRI (Fig.1)48. Regions are considered functionally connected when their BOLD time series exhibit strong statistical dependence, commonly quantiﬁed using correlation, coherence, or related measures. Large-scale functional networks are then identiﬁed using analytical approaches such as independent component analysis (ICA), clustering or graph-based methods, and seed-basedconnectivity analyses. Interactions within and between networks are typ ically examined using functional connectivity (FC) matrices that quan tify correlations among regional or network-level time series. These FC measures are often modeled as a function of age to characterize developmental and aging trajectories 49,50. They are also examined in relation to cognitive and behavioral phenotypes to understand how the progressive reorganization of functional networks supports functional specialization and integration, ultimately shaping behavioral performance across the lifespan 51. Functional connectivity within large-scale brain networks undergoes substantial age-dependent changes, beginning in utero and continuing through late adulthood52. Functional segregation, the relative dominance of within-network connectivity over between-network connectivity, reﬂects how distinct and specialized a brain n etwork is based on its connectivity patterns53. Previous studies show that functional segregation follows an inverted U-shaped trajectory across the lifespan49. It initially increases as large-scale networks rapidly differen tiate, and subsequently declines as inter-network coupling strengthens with advancing age, re ﬂecting progressive network dedifferentiation49,54,55. An extensive body of work has examined the emergence, differentiation, and reﬁnement of resting-state functional brain networks across the lifespan56–58. These networks include regions supporting primary sensory and motor functions (e.g., visual, auditory, and somatomotor processing), which emerge early in life, as well as higher-order association functions (e.g., cognition, emotion, and memory), which mature more gradually over time 59,60. Primary networks can be reliably identiﬁed in neonates and infants. In particular, somatomotor and auditory networks exhibit relatively stable within-network connectivity during theﬁrst postnatal year 61. In contrast, primary visual networks (V1 and V2) continue to strengthen their withinnetwork connectivity during this period, reﬂecting ongoing maturation and reﬁnement of visual network organization 61. Higher-order association networks, including the default mode, frontoparietal, and salience networks, are largely fragmented at birth61,62.D u r i n gt h eﬁrst few years of life, these networks gradually consolidate through the establishment of long-range connections, increased within-network connectivity, and re ﬁnement of inter-network boundaries46,53,63,64.
